title: Scope-safe language standard
note: Faculty should reinforce the distinction between triage support, patient education, and diagnosis at every stage of delivery.

title: Documentation quality threshold
note: Documentation tasks should be assessed against completeness, neutrality, and handover usefulness rather than sales style or personality.

title: Volume 1 Week 1 — faculty delivery
note: Source: IIOHR Consultant & Nurse Teaching Manual Volume 1 (Week 1). Encourage discussion of how commercial pressure can distort early patient communication. Compare patient journeys in high-trust versus high-pressure clinics. Use the manual practice activity: journey map from first enquiry to follow-up with five failure points and corrective actions.

title: Volume 1 Week 2 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Teaching Manual Volume 1 (Week 2: scope, ethics, professional responsibility). Use role-play: learners hear unsafe phrases aloud and rewrite them in calmer, bounded language. Classify phrases as within scope, borderline, or outside scope.

title: Volume 1 Week 3 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Teaching Manual Volume 1 (Week 3). Pair science with photographs or diagrams in live teaching. Emphasise translation into plain language, not jargon memorisation. Optional: collect a 300-word model answer for the thinning-despite-hairs exercise.

title: Volume 1 Week 4 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Teaching Manual Volume 1 (Week 4). Use ambiguous cases, not only obvious patterns. Normalise disciplined uncertainty and appropriate escalation. Six-case worksheet: pattern, red flags, routine vs urgent review, one patient-facing sentence for next step.

title: Volume 2 Week 5 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Teaching Manual Volume 2 (Week 5: consultation structure and history taking). Live teach the beginning–middle–end arc; open with who/role/plan. Use mock consults and mark notes for factual tone, goals versus fears, and absence of premature treatment closing. Manual practice: template with opening, history, visual concerns, treatment history, red flags, goals, handover—then mock case and revise.

title: Volume 2 Week 6 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Volume 2 Week 6 (screening, suitability, escalation). Classify cases as straightforward, cautionary, or high priority for doctor review. Drill deferral language that protects trust. Manual exercise: four mock histories, classification, rationale, one-paragraph handover each.

title: Volume 2 Week 7 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Volume 2 Week 7 (communication and expectation management). Treat communication as a safety tool. Language drill: ten risky statements rewritten to measured wording; practise aloud. Discuss warmth versus false certainty.

title: Volume 2 Week 8 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Volume 2 Week 8 (treatment education for non-doctor roles). Pathway exercise: three summaries (non-surgical-first, surgery-plus-maintenance, delayed pending review). Reinforce educate broadly, defer specifically for medications. Note: broad surgical principles in the manual continue in the program’s Surgical Pathway Education module.

title: Surgical pathway education (non-doctor) — faculty delivery
note: Bridge after Volume 2 Week 8: teach labelled pathway stages (information, doctor-led assessment and planning, procedure day logistics, early aftercare themes as general education only) without graft counts, technique-as-suitability verdicts, anaesthetic advice, or consent closure. Role-play unsafe patient lines (compare two techniques, confirm candidacy, rush booking) and rewrite to boundary-safe responses. Link forward: accurate process explanation makes Volume 3 documentation and handover easier because expectations are not poisoned pre-doctor.

title: Volume 3 Week 9 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Teaching Manual Volume 3 (Week 9). Run weak vs strong case file comparison: missing angles, vague wording, note–image mismatch, copy-paste. Practical: mock photo checklist + one-page summary + handover; rewrite narrative-heavy note into doctor-facing summary. Reinforce evidence integrity—no informal messaging as substitute for clinic notes.

title: Volume 3 Week 10 — faculty delivery
note: Source: Volume 3 Week 10. Teach sequencing over instruction dumps. Exercise: week-before-treatment reminder cadence with escalation points; plain-language pre-op checklist. Distinguish routine nerves vs unresolved clinical doubt—slow down when needed.

title: Volume 3 Week 11 (follow-up) — faculty delivery
note: Source: Volume 3 Week 11 (post-operative support and follow-up). Scenario pairs: emotionally distressed but medically routine vs calm patient with potentially abnormal symptoms—separate emotional intensity from clinical urgency. Drill message responses and follow-up notes documenting concern, response, escalation decision.

title: Volume 3 Week 11 (difficult cases) — faculty delivery
note: Source: Volume 3 Week 11 (complaints, dissatisfaction, difficult conversations—continued in this LMS module). No arguing down distress; no promising outcomes to calm; escalate when dissatisfaction may reflect complication or severe distress. Complaint prevention starts with first difficult conversation.

title: Volume 3 Week 12 (QA & systems) — faculty delivery
note: Source: Volume 3 Week 12 (systems, audit, leadership). Mini audit on sample notes, handovers, follow-up messages. Frame audit as training signal and system design question: what made error easy or success repeatable? Leadership without title: model language, standardise tools, support juniors, encourage escalation when something feels wrong.

title: Volume 3 Week 12 (capstone) — faculty delivery
note: Source: Volume 3 Week 12 final integration. Capstone should simulate clinic: intake summary, case concerns, communication response, handover prep, explicit justify escalation vs watchful waiting. Judge applied capability, usable documentation, mature tone—not memorised scripts. Ethical judgement and scope non-negotiable.

title: Capstone moderation note
note: Capstone review should confirm integrated use of scope, escalation, communication, and documentation principles before final award recommendation.
